title: The American suburbs are better than you think
gist: Noah Smith argues, using commute-time, loneliness, and migration data, that American suburbs are unfairly maligned by urbanists — commutes are actually shorter than in dense transit cities, suburbanites aren't lonelier, and Millennials are increasingly choosing suburbs for child-rearing, so the US should build more of both great cities and great suburbs rather than treating the debate as zero-sum.

themes.theme: Urban amenity demand and rent pressure

insights.claim: Manhattan apartments rent for about $5,300/month and Brooklyn for about $4,350/month, reflecting demand for urban living that increasingly exceeds job-driven relocation alone.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: High and rising urban core rents signal persistent excess demand for dense city living relative to supply — a structural tailwind for high-density residential/retail real estate in supply-constrained cities.
insights.locator: Rents in New York City are absolutely insane

insights.claim: Couture and Handbury (2020) find that desire for proximity to restaurants and nightlife ('amenities') explains about 40% of the trend of young, high-earning, college-educated people moving to cities in recent decades.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Amenity-driven migration, not just job clustering, is a major structural demand driver for dense urban cores — relevant to valuing urban retail/hospitality real estate and predicting which cities retain young talent.
insights.locator: Couture and Handbury (2020) find that wanting to be close to restaurants

insights.claim: NYC has one of the lowest violent crime rates among big US cities, contradicting the stereotype that dense cities are crime-ridden and unsafe.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Undercuts a common bearish narrative about urban core safety that sometimes weighs on urban real estate sentiment.
insights.locator: the stereotype of dense cities as crime-ridden and unsafe is just wrong

insights.claim: Despite greater sprawl, Americans have some of the shortest average commute times in the developed world according to OECD data, because US metro areas are polycentric (jobs are dispersed, not concentrated in one central business district) and workers tend to live near where they work.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Rebuts the common thesis that suburban sprawl imposes a large commute-time tax — relevant when modeling household time-cost tradeoffs for suburban vs. urban living decisions.
insights.locator: Despite greater sprawl, Americans have some of the shortest commutes

insights.claim: Liao et al. (2020) find that using public transit takes on average 1.4–2.6 times longer than driving a car across cities including São Paulo, Sydney, Stockholm, and Amsterdam — even in cities built to be transit-friendly and even accounting for parking time.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Cars retain a durable time-cost advantage over transit globally, which structurally supports continued car-dependent suburban development even in transit-oriented markets.
insights.quote: using PT takes on average 1.4–2.6 times longer than driving a car
insights.locator: We use real-world data to make realistic estimates of travel time

insights.claim: Car ownership functions as a form of wealth and a desired good in itself (privacy, convenience, freedom) rather than merely a cost suburbanites are forced to bear in exchange for a big house.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Reframes car demand as a durable consumer preference rather than a distortion, relevant to long-run auto demand and suburban housing demand forecasting.
insights.locator: Car ownership is a form of wealth

themes.theme: Suburbs are not lonelier than cities

insights.claim: Abshire et al. (2022), surveying 616 US adults, found suburban living was associated with lower odds of loneliness than urban living (loneliness prevalence: 40.8% suburban vs. 54.3% urban), though not statistically significant after adjustment.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Directly contradicts the popular 'suburbs are isolating' narrative with survey evidence — relevant for evaluating social/mental-health framing used in pro-density policy advocacy.
insights.quote: The prevalence of loneliness was 50.7%, 59.0%, 40.8%, and 54.3% in small rural, large rural, suburban, and urban areas, respectively.

insights.claim: Hammond et al. (2021), tracking 756 participants across 16,602 momentary assessments, found increased overcrowding and population density were associated with higher loneliness, while social inclusivity and nature contact were associated with lower loneliness, even after controlling for demographics.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Suggests density itself can be a loneliness risk factor, cutting against simplistic pro-density social-wellbeing arguments.

insights.claim: Morris and Pfeiffer (2016), using 2003–2013 American Time Use Survey data, found no meaningful difference between suburbanites' and city dwellers' time spent socializing after controlling for personal characteristics; Bower et al. (2022) reviewed 57 studies and found no systematic association between loneliness and any measured feature of the built environment.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Multiple independent studies converge on no built-environment effect on loneliness — a robust empirical rebuttal to the 'suburbs cause isolation' claim often used in urbanist policy arguments.
insights.quote: we find no meaningful difference in suburbanites’ and city dwellers’ time spent socializing
insights.locator: Based on data from the 2003 to 2013 American Time Use Surveys

insights.claim: What actually reduces loneliness is repeated interaction with people you already know and care about, not simply the frequency of passing strangers on the street — suburban homes with more space for entertaining, plus shorter drive times to visit friends, can facilitate this better than small urban apartments.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Offers a causal mechanism explaining the loneliness survey data — useful for interpreting future social-wellbeing research on urban form.
insights.locator: Why doesn’t the simple intuition work here?

themes.theme: The suburbanization trend is accelerating, led by families

insights.claim: The urban revival of the 1990s-2010s was modest and driven mainly by young, high-earning, educated people; in recent years the trend toward suburbanization has accelerated, and per Jed Kolko's data, more than 100% of recent dense-core rebound is attributable to immigration, while domestic migration continues to move away from city centers.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Domestic-only migration flows are net negative for dense urban cores — a key data point for forecasting urban vs. suburban housing demand and city population/tax-base trends.
insights.locator: Kolko shows that although the densest city centers are rebounding

insights.claim: The Joint Center for Housing Studies finds that throughout the past decade Millennials were moving to suburbs farther out from the city center, with the largest population increases among early Millennials occurring in both suburban and outer-metro peripheral areas, despite widespread discussion of millennial preference for walkable urban areas.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Contradicts the 'Millennials prefer urban walkability' narrative that has shaped a decade of urban real estate and retail investment theses — suggests continued suburban housing demand from this cohort as it ages.
insights.quote: we found that the places with the largest increases of early millennials were both suburban and on the periphery of metropolitan areas
insights.locator: This is from the Joint Center for Housing Studies

insights.claim: Albouy and Faberman (2025) find high-skill workers sort into high-amenity (urban) areas early in life but move toward lower-amenity, lower-cost metro areas during their thirties and forties, with this shift specifically driven by having a first child, particularly among the college-educated.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Establishes a predictable, child-driven life-cycle migration pattern from cities to suburbs — a durable demand driver for suburban single-family housing tied to demographic/fertility trends, not a fad.
insights.quote: individuals, particularly the college educated, tend to move towards lower-amenity areas after having their first child
insights.locator: Albouy and Faberman (2025) find that the kind of high-skilled workers

insights.claim: Jed Kolko's data shows people with a child over age 6 tended to move out of cities during the 1990s-2010s urban boomlet, even as childless people and those with young children continued moving in.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Refines the urban-to-suburban migration timing: the exodus concentrates around school-age children, informing school-district-linked suburban housing demand forecasts.
insights.locator: Kolko shows something similar — people with a kid over 6

themes.theme: Policy framing: build both, not either/or

insights.claim: Smith argues the US should build a few more Manhattans and a lot more Brooklyns — increasing both dense urban core supply and suburban supply — since demand for dense city living currently exceeds willingness to build it, artificially inflating rents and pushing unwilling people into suburbs.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Frames the optimal policy stance as supply expansion across both housing typologies rather than density-only YIMBYism — relevant for evaluating which housing/YIMBY policy proposals are likely to gain broader political support.
insights.quote: Basically, everyone would be happy if America had a few more Manhattans and a lot more Brooklyns.
insights.locator: The demand for life in cities like NYC exceeds America’s willingness to supply

insights.claim: Smith concedes real suburban downsides: low-density sprawl is expensive to maintain, tends to reduce walking and health, and reduces retail/restaurant variety because a critical mass of nearby population is needed to support niche businesses.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Acknowledges genuine suburban infrastructure cost and retail-variety limitations, tempering a pure pro-suburb reading and pointing to 'gentle density' (rowhouses, duplexes, small apartment buildings, bike lanes, commuter rail) as the preferred incremental policy path.
insights.locator: Low-density sprawl is very expensive to maintain
